title: Dealing with new data
text: still working Belgian SARI data described vignette Getting started (vignette("getting-started")). simulate 40 extra weeks data consisting single peak: 20 weeks incidence increases linearly followed 20 weeks drops rate. add random noise make look like real world data. first simulate normal scenario new peak similar height 2 previous seasons. create data set df_sim_normal new data, add old data create df_all_normal. One way incorporate new data recompute thresholds. can add data week per week using run_loop_thresholds() demonstrated Getting started (vignette("getting-started")). expected, thresholds remain stable.  Next simulate high scenario new peak higher 2 previous seasons. see thresholds increase account intense new season. high high thresholds impacted , since sensitive highest observed values.
code: simulate_rates <- function(rate_increase) {   bind_rows(     # Increasing rate over 20 weeks     tibble(       index = as.Date("2025-05-26") + 1:20 * 7,       rate = 8 + rate_increase * 1:20 + rnorm(20)     ),     # Falling rate over 20 weeks     tibble(       index = as.Date("2025-05-26") + 21:40 * 7,       rate = 8 + rate_increase * 20 - rate_increase * 1:20 + rnorm(20)     )   ) } df_sim_normal <- simulate_rates(rate_increase = .7) df_all_normal <- bind_rows(df_sari_be, df_sim_normal) fit_loop_normal <- run_loop_thresholds(df_all_normal, n_states = 3) loop_plots_normal <- create_loop_plots(fit_loop_normal) loop_plots_normal$thresholds +   geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-05-26"), linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) df_sim_high <- simulate_rates(rate_increase = 1) df_all_high <- bind_rows(df_sari_be, df_sim_high) fit_loop_high <- run_loop_thresholds(df_all_high, n_states = 3) loop_plots_high <- create_loop_plots(fit_loop_high) loop_plots_high$thresholds +   geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-05-26"), linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1)

title: Dealing with new data
text: following approach incorporating new data line threshold computation, routinely done per year. Freezing hidden Markov model used compute thresholds, can use decode weeks new season state. stress HMM frozen new data used retrain model potentially redefine hidden states. literature refers process --sample decoding, highlighting new data part sample model trained. run HMM compute thresholds original data . decode state assignments old new data using states hmm_frozen. several ways decode states, discuss without going technical details. One global way assign states answer following question: given fitted HMM, possible combinations state assignments week, likely given observed incidences? encoded method = "global" output run_out_of_sample_decoding().  second approach look week separately determine state. assignment based observed incidence week (likely emitted distribution state?) assigned states preceding following week (likely state transition?). Rather outputting single state week, given distribution different states week. encoded method = "local" provides called posterior probabilities Getting started (vignette("getting-started")). want one single state assignment week, can take probable one.  third approach, encoded method = "filtering" exactly method = "local", takes account preceding week, following week. may seem strange take account available information, state assignments stable sense adding one week data change second--last state assignment.  output run_out_of_sample_decoding() contains two data.frame objects, one probable state according 3 methods one state probabilities filtering local methods.
code: fit_frozen <- run_hmm(df_sari_be, n_states = 3, type = "rate") thresh <- run_threshold_computation(fit_frozen) decode_states_normal <- run_out_of_sample_decoding(   obs_data_new = df_sim_normal,    hmm_frozen = fit_frozen ) decode_plots_normal <- create_decoding_plots(decode_states_normal, method = "global") decode_plots_normal$time_series +   geom_hline(yintercept = thresh$thresholds, linetype = "dashed") decode_plots_normal <- create_decoding_plots(decode_states_normal, method = "local") decode_plots_normal$time_series +   geom_hline(yintercept = thresh$thresholds, linetype = "dashed") decode_plots_normal <- create_decoding_plots(decode_states_normal, method = "filtering") decode_plots_normal$time_series +   geom_hline(yintercept = thresh$thresholds, linetype = "dashed") head(decode_plots_normal$states, 12) #> NULL head(decode_plots_normal$probs, 12) #> NULL

title: Getting started with epiquest
text: data need specific format order use functions epiquest package. surveillance time series needs stored data.frame following columns: index column Date integer column. rate column count incidence (numeric). use Belgian SARI data (included package df_sari_be) demonstration. data plotted . vertical dotted line indicates timing change surveillance system: November 13, 2023, new, wider SARI case definition adopted. hospitals still reported whether patients satisfied old, narrow case definition, possible investigate relationship old new case definition incidences. linear regression model provided excellent fit. data November 13, 2023, old case definition incidences transformed new case definition scale using linear model. data since November 13, 2023, observed new case definition incidences.
code: head(df_sari_be) #>        index     rate #> 1 2021-06-21 4.780023 #> 2 2021-06-28 7.867652 #> 3 2021-07-05 7.190655 #> 4 2021-07-12 8.544648 #> 5 2021-07-19 2.451679 #> 6 2021-07-26 9.221645 ggplot(df_sari_be, aes(x = index, y = rate)) +   geom_point() + geom_line() +   geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2023-11-13"), linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) +   labs(x = "Index", y = "Rate", title = "SARI incidence in Belgium") +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA))

title: Getting started with epiquest
text: Reading tip: easy task understand (explain, matter) hidden Markov model . Instead opting rigorous exposition, gently talk relevant output functions run_hmm() create_hmm_plots() hope undestand HMM end section. first step fit hidden Markov model (HMM) surveillance time series. set n_states = 3, assume epidemiological situation week 1 3 (hidden) states. argument type = rate indicates data incidences/counts, rather percentages (type = perc). diving technical mechanics HMM, jump ahead end provide intuition headed. plot shows time series week colored probable state. may note different states different incidences. may also note state changes week week relatively rare. words, HMM identifies hidden states effectively clustering data across time dimension incidence dimension. categorizing data way, can use one () hidden states data-driven definition epidemic window.  dive details . summary() function already provides overview important information HMM fit. state distribution incidences. function run_hmm() assumes distributions normal/Gaussian type = rate. can see estimated mean standard deviation 3 hidden states named L1, L2 L3. states always ordered increasing mean: L1 low activity state mean incidence 4.47. L2 medium activity state mean incidence 10.76. L3 high activity state mean incidence 18.01. incidences form one important aspect hidden Markov model called emission. underlying idea different states emit different kinds incidences. Part variation incidence throughout year (baseline incidences low activity weeks peak incidences high activity weeks) explained difference hidden state. second important aspect transition: starting initial state first week, HMM models transition states one week next. Check diagonal transition matrix , 3 states, probability staying state around 90% higher. addition, see transitions directly low activity state L1 high activity state L3 unlikely. Instead, transition occur indirectly medium activity state L2. byproduct estimating emission distributions transition probabilities, HMM provides information hidden state week. course uncertainty states, model provides probability distribution1 (-called posterior probabilities) across states rather single ‘hard’ classification. time series plot visualizes results, observation colored according probable state.  next plots illustrate uncertainty model state assignments. first one, state probabilities plotted week. Note blocks time (little) uncertainty state assignment. hand, observe uncertainty occurs transitioning one state next.  facet plot shows time series 3 times, state. observations shaded probability state question. observation can appear twice plot: 90% probability L2 (medium activity/yellow) 10% probability L3 (high activity/red), appear almost fully yellow middle facet faintly red top facet. Note quite shaded observations high activity L3 state.  Finally, see histogram observed incidences state, overlayed fitted normal distributions. Actually, histogram weighted posterior probabilities - much like previous visualization - observation can contribute partially one state.  see overlap emission distribution state. separated HMM? example, make sense reassign highest incidences observed L2 state (yellow) L3 state (red)? may seem better incidence perspective, reassigning weeks different state introduce state switching. balancing clustering incidences clustering time, model allows overlap indicence distributions avoids excessing state switching favors extended periods stable state assignments. last remarks concerning missing data: - function run_hmm() can handle missing observations; removed data, rather left NA. - However, surveillance signal interrupted extended periods (e.g., systems operate low intensity months), strongly recommended use seasonal = TRUE run_hmm(). case, weeks data collected removed. grouping variable season must included indicate weeks belong season. HMM treat seasonal subseries independent time series.
code: fit <- run_hmm(df_sari_be, n_states = 3, type = "rate") hmm_plots <- create_hmm_plots(fit) hmm_plots$time_series_full summary(fit) #>  #> ======================================================== #>          EpiQUEST hidden Markov model summary            #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------- #> Type:                    Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:        3  #> Seasonal:                FALSE  #> Number of observations:  206  #>  #> --- Estimated state parameters ------------------------- #>  State   Mean Standard deviation #>     L1  4.468              1.722 #>     L2 10.758              2.159 #>     L3 18.011              2.641 #>  #> --- Transition matrix ---------------------------------- #>   State   ToL1   ToL2   ToL3 #>  FromL1 95.73%  4.27%  0.00% #>  FromL2  2.51% 91.01%  6.48% #>  FromL3  0.00% 11.32% 88.68% #>  #> --- State distribution (observations) ------------------ #>  State Total weight Proportion #>     L1         72.6      35.2% #>     L2         85.2      41.4% #>     L3         48.2      23.4% #>  #> Note: Weights are posterior probabilities. #> ======================================================== fit$states #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   state mean_state sd_state #>   <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 L1          4.47     1.72 #> 2 L2         10.8      2.16 #> 3 L3         18.0      2.64 round(fit$transition, 2) #>        ToL1 ToL2 ToL3 #> FromL1 0.96 0.04 0.00 #> FromL2 0.03 0.91 0.06 #> FromL3 0.00 0.11 0.89 hmm_plots$time_series_full hmm_plots$prob_states hmm_plots$time_series_per_state hmm_plots$histogram_soft

title: Getting started with epiquest
text: essential expert familiar surveillance system review model output verify whether one () states truly align high burden operational definition epidemic. expert validation ensures statistical transitions identified model reflect practical realities pressures faced healthcare system, HMM course take account. practice, model n_states = 2 n_states = 3 fitted compared. hidden state highest mean incidence likely candidate epidemic state. can foresee exceptions, however: - data exhibit high peaks, model may split (expert think ) epidemic period two separate states, showing difference high burden extremely high one. cases, states together might used define full epidemic. - vein, data exhibit single high (super-epidemic) peak, smaller peaks seasons, one states may capture extreme incidence super-epidemic peak. circumstances surrounding peak thought unlikely occur , super-epidemic state can excluded definition normal epidemic.

title: Getting started with epiquest
text: epidemic state identified, QUEST thresholds can computed using run_threshold_computation(). default option state highest mean incidence epidemic state. default can overwritten using epidemic_state_incidences argument (illustrated ). threshold computation straightforward: low, medium, high high thresholds 5%, 70%, 90% 99% quantile, respectively, distribution observed incidences, weighted posterior probability epidemic state. exactly distribution visualized (hmm_plots$histogram_soft). Said another way: simply want define thresholds quantiles observed incidences epidemic state. Since HMM definitively determine weeks state, rather uses soft probabilistic approach, allow incidences partially contribute distribution observed incidences epidemic state using posterior probabilities weights. function create_threshold_plots() recreates plots previously discussed, computed thresholds added. see low threshold reasonably separates medium activity (L2) high activity (L3) states, except outliers L2 state.  none L2 outliers last 2 seasons, can see .  QUEST thresholds straightforward interpret. Take medium threshold example, computed 70% quantile observed epidemic state incidences. - Surpassing medium threshold current week means incidence top 30% epidemic incidences historic data. - statement begs question epidemic incidence : definition epidemic data-driven, output statistical procedure clusters time incidence epidemic weeks part sustained period incidence high. definition epidemic incidence much bigger impact low threshold ones. high threshold heavily influenced highest observation, hand.
code: thresh <- run_threshold_computation(fit) summary(thresh) #>  #> ============================================================== #>         EpiQUEST threshold summary                             #> ============================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------------- #> Type:                          Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:              3  #> Seasonal:                      FALSE  #> Number of observations:        206  #> State(s) defined as epidemic:  L3  #>  #> --- Calculated QUEST thresholds ------------------------------ #>      Level Quantile  Value #>        Low       5% 13.881 #>     Medium      70% 19.734 #>       High      90% 21.408 #>  Very high      99% 22.647 #>  #> Note: Thresholds calculated using weighted ECDF #> based on posterior probabilities of epidemic state(s). #> ============================================================== thresh_plots <- create_threshold_plots(thresh) thresh_plots$histogram_soft thresh_plots$time_series_full

title: Getting started with epiquest
text: thresholds meaningfully change one fewer week data? check stable thresholds HMM states running model increasingly large window data. start initial window 50 weeks gradually additional data one week time. procedure may run … thresholds change meaningfully dataset slightly different - example, one fewer week data? evaluate threshold HMM stability recomputing thresholds expanding window data, starting initial 50-week period adding data one week time. plot shows evolution parameters incidence distribution (mean standard deviation normal distribution) state data window expands. see clear break: initially HMM difficulty identifying states, significantly changing mean standard deviation states one week data added. Eventually state assignments stabilize, changing smoothly new data added.  may easier check stability mean standard deviation jointly. plot mean every state, together dark shaded band one standard deviation mean light shaded band two standard deviations mean.  Finally, can check stability thresholds . draw conclusions, notably thresholds react relatively smoothly addition new data last couple months data.  summary() function provides information thresholds mean HMM states past 12 iterations. states thresholds unstable, consider changing number states. slightly unstable (.e., fluctuations reasonably small), can consider taking median computed thresholds last 12 iterations stability analysis.
code: fit_loop <- run_loop_thresholds(df_sari_be, n_states = 3) loop_plots <- create_loop_plots(fit_loop) loop_plots$states_facet loop_plots$states loop_plots$thresholds summary(fit_loop) #>  #> ======================================================== #>         EpiQUEST stability analysis summary              #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Loop configuration --------------------------------- #> Number of iterations (refits):  156  #> Step size (increments):         7 units #> Summary window:                 12 last iterations #>  #> --- Threshold stability (in summary window) ------------ #>       type   Median     Mean      Min      Max #>        Low 13.89241 13.88527 13.81050 13.89924 #>     Medium 19.81590 19.82544 19.73403 19.93247 #>       High 21.41031 21.41018 21.40796 21.41212 #>  Very high 22.67035 22.66909 22.64746 22.68797 #>  #> --- State mean stability (in summary window) ----------- #>  state  Median      Mean    Min    Max #>     L1  4.4785  4.476667  4.468  4.482 #>     L2 10.9540 10.928250 10.758 11.034 #>     L3 18.1250 18.115000 18.011 18.208 #>  #> For information on standard deviation stability and #> overlap, please look at output of create_loop_plots(). #> ========================================================

title: Getting started with epiquest
text: Recall data preparation step changes made surveillance system November 2023. may noticed low activity L1 state mainly used low season peaks change.  happen just used data change model 2 states? investigate . Note means new L1 L2 states similar means old L2 L3 states. terms probable states, classification identical old one.  thresholds comparable methods, biggest impact low threshold. 2-state model recent data much stable, see .   exercise shows QUEST thresholds, despite data-driven definition epidemic, can produce stable results, even two (incomplete) seasons data.
code: hmm_plots$time_series_full +   geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2023-11-13"), linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) df_sari_be_new <- df_sari_be %>%    filter(index >= as.Date("2023-11-13")) fit_new <- run_hmm(df_sari_be_new, n_states = 2) summary(fit_new) #>  #> ======================================================== #>          EpiQUEST hidden Markov model summary            #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------- #> Type:                    Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:        2  #> Seasonal:                FALSE  #> Number of observations:  81  #>  #> --- Estimated state parameters ------------------------- #>  State   Mean Standard deviation #>     L1 10.582              1.922 #>     L2 18.359              2.395 #>  #> --- Transition matrix ---------------------------------- #>   State   ToL1   ToL2 #>  FromL1 97.85%  2.15% #>  FromL2  6.01% 93.99% #>  #> --- State distribution (observations) ------------------ #>  State Total weight Proportion #>     L1         47.7      58.8% #>     L2         33.3      41.2% #>  #> Note: Weights are posterior probabilities. #> ======================================================== hmm_plots_new <- create_hmm_plots(fit_new) hmm_plots_new$time_series_full thresh_new <- run_threshold_computation(fit_new) tibble(   Threshold = c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very high"),   Old = thresh$threshold,   New = thresh_new$thresholds ) #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   Threshold   Old   New #>   <chr>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Low        13.9  14.4 #> 2 Medium     19.7  19.8 #> 3 High       21.4  21.4 #> 4 Very high  22.6  22.9 fit_loop_new <- run_loop_thresholds(df_sari_be_new, n_states = 2) loop_plots_new <- create_loop_plots(fit_loop_new) loop_plots_new$thresholds loop_plots_new$states_facet

what: Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots

title: Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots
Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots
text: list_results object class epiquest_hmm produced run_hmm(). print logical. TRUE, generated plots printed active graphics device.

title: Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots
text: object class epiquest_plot_list, named list ggplot2 objects containing: jitter_hard/soft Jitter plot showing signal distribution per state. histogram_hard/soft Histogram showing signal density per state. prob_states Stacked bar chart posterior state probabilities. time_series_per_state Faceted time series state. time_series_full Single time series colored probable state.

title: Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots
text: function produces several types visualizations: Time series: raw signal colored probable state. Jitter plots histograms: Visualize overlap separation states. State probabilities: stacked bar chart showing certainty state assignments time. many plots, "hard" "soft" versions provided. Hard assignments classify time point strictly single state highest posterior probability. Soft assignments weight observation probability belonging state, providing nuanced view uncertainty.

title: Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots
text: list_results$type == "rate", histogram_soft plot overlays estimated Gaussian density curves state.

title: Visualize hidden Markov model results — create_hmm_plots
code: # Fit a 3-state HMM to (continuous) rate data fit <- run_hmm(df_sari_be, n_states = 3, type = "rate")  # Check state information summary(fit) #>  #> ======================================================== #>          EpiQUEST hidden Markov model summary            #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------- #> Type:                    Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:        3  #> Seasonal:                FALSE  #> Number of observations:  206  #>  #> --- Estimated state parameters ------------------------- #>  State   Mean Standard deviation #>     L1  4.468              1.722 #>     L2 10.758              2.159 #>     L3 18.011              2.641 #>  #> --- Transition matrix ---------------------------------- #>   State   ToL1   ToL2   ToL3 #>  FromL1 95.73%  4.27%  0.00% #>  FromL2  2.51% 91.01%  6.48% #>  FromL3  0.00% 11.32% 88.68% #>  #> --- State distribution (observations) ------------------ #>  State Total weight Proportion #>     L1         72.6      35.2% #>     L2         85.2      41.4% #>     L3         48.2      23.4% #>  #> Note: Weights are posterior probabilities. #> ========================================================  # Visualize state information create_hmm_plots(fit)         # Compute thresholds using the highest state (L3) as the epidemic state # By default, epidemic_state_indices is the highest state thresh <- run_threshold_computation(fit)  # Visualize theshold information summary(thresh) #>  #> ============================================================== #>         EpiQUEST threshold summary                             #> ============================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------------- #> Type:                          Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:              3  #> Seasonal:                      FALSE  #> Number of observations:        206  #> State(s) defined as epidemic:  L3  #>  #> --- Calculated QUEST thresholds ------------------------------ #>      Level Quantile  Value #>        Low       5% 13.881 #>     Medium      70% 19.734 #>       High      90% 21.408 #>  Very high      99% 22.647 #>  #> Note: Thresholds calculated using weighted ECDF #> based on posterior probabilities of epidemic state(s). #> ==============================================================  # Visualize theshold information create_threshold_plots(thresh)

code: # Fit a 3-state HMM to (continuous) rate data fit <- run_hmm(df_sari_be, n_states = 3, type = "rate")  # Check state information summary(fit) #>  #> ======================================================== #>          EpiQUEST hidden Markov model summary            #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------- #> Type:                    Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:        3  #> Seasonal:                FALSE  #> Number of observations:  206  #>  #> --- Estimated state parameters ------------------------- #>  State   Mean Standard deviation #>     L1  4.468              1.722 #>     L2 10.758              2.159 #>     L3 18.011              2.641 #>  #> --- Transition matrix ---------------------------------- #>   State   ToL1   ToL2   ToL3 #>  FromL1 95.73%  4.27%  0.00% #>  FromL2  2.51% 91.01%  6.48% #>  FromL3  0.00% 11.32% 88.68% #>  #> --- State distribution (observations) ------------------ #>  State Total weight Proportion #>     L1         72.6      35.2% #>     L2         85.2      41.4% #>     L3         48.2      23.4% #>  #> Note: Weights are posterior probabilities. #> ========================================================  # Visualize state information create_hmm_plots(fit)         # Compute thresholds using the highest state (L3) as the epidemic state # By default, epidemic_state_indices is the highest state thresh <- run_threshold_computation(fit)  # Visualize theshold information summary(thresh) #>  #> ============================================================== #>         EpiQUEST threshold summary                             #> ============================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------------- #> Type:                          Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:              3  #> Seasonal:                      FALSE  #> Number of observations:        206  #> State(s) defined as epidemic:  L3  #>  #> --- Calculated QUEST thresholds ------------------------------ #>      Level Quantile  Value #>        Low       5% 13.881 #>     Medium      70% 19.734 #>       High      90% 21.408 #>  Very high      99% 22.647 #>  #> Note: Thresholds calculated using weighted ECDF #> based on posterior probabilities of epidemic state(s). #> ==============================================================  # Visualize theshold information create_threshold_plots(thresh)

what: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
title: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
text: function fits hidden Markov model (HMM) time series data using either Gaussian (rates) binomial (percentages) distributions.

title: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
text: obs_data data.frame containing index column (Date integer). Additional required columns depend type seasonal: type = "rate": Must contain numeric rate column. represents intensity, incidence activity level surveillance signal (e.g., cases per 100,000 population). type = "perc": Must contain integer num denom columns. num numerator (e.g., number positive lab tests symptomatic patients), denom denominator total sample size (e.g., total tests performed total clinical consultations). seasonal = TRUE: Must contain season column. subseries season treated independent time series. n_states integer. number hidden states (2 4). type character string. Either "rate" (Gaussian) "perc" (binomial). seasonal logical. TRUE, model prevents transitions different seasons, treating independent sequences.

title: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
text: object class epiquest_hmm. list containing: data: input obs_data added columns predicted state posterior probabilities. states: Summary statistics (mean SD, probability) hidden state. transition: estimated transition matrix states. n_states: Number states model. type: data type used fit.

title: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
text: function uses depmixS4 package estimate parameters using Baum-Welch algorithm. type = "perc", model accounts varying precision proportions using raw counts (num denom). fitting, hidden states sorted renamed L1 (lowest intensity) Ln (highest intensity) based estimated mean. posterior probabilities 'local' probabilities. See run_out_of_sample_decoding information.

title: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
text: Missing values (NA) permitted response columns (rate num/denom). depmixS4 handles allowing state transitions occur usual ignoring missing observation likelihood calculation. However, surveillance signal interrupted extended periods (e.g., systems operate low intensity months), strongly recommended use seasonal = TRUE.

title: Fit a hidden Markov model (HMM) to epidemiological time series data — run_hmm
code: # Fit a 3-state HMM to (continuous) rate data fit <- run_hmm(df_sari_be, n_states = 3, type = "rate")  # Check state information summary(fit) #>  #> ======================================================== #>          EpiQUEST hidden Markov model summary            #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------- #> Type:                    Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:        3  #> Seasonal:                FALSE  #> Number of observations:  206  #>  #> --- Estimated state parameters ------------------------- #>  State   Mean Standard deviation #>     L1  4.468              1.722 #>     L2 10.758              2.159 #>     L3 18.011              2.641 #>  #> --- Transition matrix ---------------------------------- #>   State   ToL1   ToL2   ToL3 #>  FromL1 95.73%  4.27%  0.00% #>  FromL2  2.51% 91.01%  6.48% #>  FromL3  0.00% 11.32% 88.68% #>  #> --- State distribution (observations) ------------------ #>  State Total weight Proportion #>     L1         72.6      35.2% #>     L2         85.2      41.4% #>     L3         48.2      23.4% #>  #> Note: Weights are posterior probabilities. #> ========================================================  # Visualize state information create_hmm_plots(fit)         # Compute thresholds using the highest state (L3) as the epidemic state # By default, epidemic_state_indices is the highest state thresh <- run_threshold_computation(fit)  # Visualize theshold information summary(thresh) #>  #> ============================================================== #>         EpiQUEST threshold summary                             #> ============================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------------- #> Type:                          Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:              3  #> Seasonal:                      FALSE  #> Number of observations:        206  #> State(s) defined as epidemic:  L3  #>  #> --- Calculated QUEST thresholds ------------------------------ #>      Level Quantile  Value #>        Low       5% 13.881 #>     Medium      70% 19.734 #>       High      90% 21.408 #>  Very high      99% 22.647 #>  #> Note: Thresholds calculated using weighted ECDF #> based on posterior probabilities of epidemic state(s). #> ==============================================================  # Visualize theshold information create_threshold_plots(thresh)

Fit HMM for binomial percentage data — run_hmm_perc

TRUE, model prevents transitions different seasons, treating independent sequences.

title: Fit HMM for binomial percentage data — run_hmm_perc

title: Fit HMM for binomial percentage data — run_hmm_perc

title: Fit HMM for continuous rate data — run_hmm_rate
Fit HMM for continuous rate data — run_hmm_rate
text: obs_data data.frame containing index column (Date integer). Additional required columns depend type seasonal: type = "rate": Must contain numeric rate column. represents intensity, incidence activity level surveillance signal (e.g., cases per 100,000 population). type = "perc": Must contain integer num denom columns. num numerator (e.g., number positive lab tests symptomatic patients), denom denominator total sample size (e.g., total tests performed total clinical consultations). seasonal = TRUE: Must contain season column. subseries season treated independent time series. n_states integer. number hidden states (2 4). seasonal logical. TRUE, model prevents transitions different seasons, treating independent sequences.

title: Fit HMM for continuous rate data — run_hmm_rate

title: Fit HMM for continuous rate data — run_hmm_rate

what: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
title: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
text: Evaluates robustness HMM states QUEST thresholds repeatedly refitting model expanding window time series.

title: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
code: run_loop_thresholds(   obs_data,   n_states = 2,   type = "rate",   seasonal = FALSE,   quantiles = c(0.05, 0.7, 0.9, 0.99),   epidemic_state_indices = NULL,   step = 7,   window_summary = 12 )

title: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
text: obs_data data.frame formatted run_hmm(). n_states integer. Number hidden states (2 4). type character string. Either "rate" (Gaussian) "perc" (binomial). seasonal logical. TRUE, treats seasons independent. quantiles numeric vector. Quantile levels threshold calculation. epidemic_state_indices integer vector. HMM states considered "epidemic" (see section 'Defining "epidemic" state(s)' run_threshold_computation). step integer. number units advance cutoff date iteration. Defaults 7 (one week). window_summary integer. number recent iterations include calculating final summary statistics.

title: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
text: object class epiquest_loop. list containing: thresholds: data frame thresholds calculated cutoff date. states: data frame showing state means standard deviations evolved. summary: summary table (median, mean, min, max) thresholds within final window_summary iterations. data: original input data.

title: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
text: Stability analysis: ensure HMM enough data converge stable estimate, function starts minimum training set 50 step units. adds data increments step (default 1 week 7 days), refitting model recalculating thresholds every stage. process mimics model perform real-time surveillance setting new data arrives week. thresholds state means show high volatility, may indicate model overfit surveillance signal noisy chosen number states.

title: Stability analysis of QUEST thresholds via iterative refitting — run_loop_thresholds
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Stability analysis takes time as it refits the model repeatedly fit_loop <- loop_thresholds(df_sari_be, n_states = 2)  # Look at results summary(fit_loop)  # Visualize results create_loop_plots(fit_loop) } # }

what: Out-of-sample state decoding using a frozen HMM — run_out_of_sample_decoding
title: Out-of-sample state decoding using a frozen HMM — run_out_of_sample_decoding
text: function applies parameters previously fitted hidden Markov model (epiquest_hmm object) new data. allows freezing HMM parameters (transition probabilities, emission distributions initial state distribution) determining hidden states new data without re-estimating model.

title: Out-of-sample state decoding using a frozen HMM — run_out_of_sample_decoding
Out-of-sample state decoding using a frozen HMM — run_out_of_sample_decoding
text: obs_data_new data.frame containing new observations. Must contain index column response columns (e.g., rate num/denom) used fit hmm_frozen. seasonal = TRUE, must also contain season column. hmm_frozen object class epiquest_hmm. fitted model object containing parameters applied new data. seasonal logical. TRUE, function respects season boundaries obs_data_new prevent transitions different seasons.

title: Out-of-sample state decoding using a frozen HMM — run_out_of_sample_decoding
text: object class epiquest_decode. list containing: states: input obs_data_new added columns predicted state method used (see Details ). probs: methods 'local' 'filtering', posterior probabilities states.

title: Out-of-sample state decoding using a frozen HMM — run_out_of_sample_decoding
text: function initializes new depmix model using structure obs_data_new uses depmixS4::setpars hard-code parameters hmm_frozen. particularly useful prospective surveillance, want classify new weekly data using model trained historical seasons without new data points influencing previously trained model. states can decoded according different methods: 'global': Uses Viterbi algorithm find single likely sequence states across entire time series. approach outputs single state data point. associated probabilities. 'local': Assigns state highest posterior smoothing probability specific time point. 'filtering': Similar 'local', performs real-time decoding, state time t determined observations time 1 t. Comparing 'local' 'filtering', approach advantages disadvantages. Filtering state assignments use information following week determine states, use available information. computed every week new data data arrive, state assignments stable. Local state assingments can change: stable data following week available. plots generated create_hmm_plots() create_loop_plots() use 'local' state assignments.

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
text: Calculates QUEST thresholds (default: low, medium, high, high) determining specific quantiles observed surveillance signal, weighted posterior probability system user-defined epidemic state.

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
code: run_threshold_computation(   list_results,   quantiles = c(0.05, 0.7, 0.9, 0.99),   epidemic_state_indices = NULL )

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
text: list_results object class epiquest_hmm produced run_hmm(). quantiles numeric vector. cumulative probabilities (0 1) calculate thresholds. Defaults c(0.05, 0.70, 0.90, 0.99). epidemic_state_indices integer vector. indices HMM states jointly considered "epidemic" signal. NULL, defaults state highest mean (see section 'Defining "epidemic" state(s)' ).

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
text: object class epiquest_thresholds, inherits epiquest_hmm. input list_results 3 additional elements: thresholds: numeric vector calculated rate values. quantiles: input quantile levels used calculation. epidemic_state_indices: indices states used define epidemic state.

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
text: function implements "soft" thresholding approach. Instead calculating quantiles raw data alone, uses HMM posterior probabilities weight observations. observation, function: Determines likely specific week "epidemic" state(s). Constructs weighted empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) observed rate. Uses linear interpolation find exact rates correspond chosen quantiles.

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
text: epidemic_state_indices argument allows define states HMM output represent "epidemic activity". flexibility useful several scenarios: Combining states: 3-state model, might consider state 2 ("Elevated") state 3 ("Epidemic") represent high activity activity. set epidemic_state_indices = c(2, 3). Excluding extreme outliers: data includes "super-epidemic" year, highest HMM state might capture rare extremes. set thresholds sensitive normal annual epidemics, might choose define epidemic signal based state second highest mean. indices provided, function defaults state highest mean incidence.

title: Compute QUEST thresholds — run_threshold_computation
code: # Fit a 3-state HMM to (continuous) rate data fit <- run_hmm(df_sari_be, n_states = 3, type = "rate")  # Check state information summary(fit) #>  #> ======================================================== #>          EpiQUEST hidden Markov model summary            #> ======================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------- #> Type:                    Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:        3  #> Seasonal:                FALSE  #> Number of observations:  206  #>  #> --- Estimated state parameters ------------------------- #>  State   Mean Standard deviation #>     L1  4.468              1.722 #>     L2 10.758              2.159 #>     L3 18.011              2.641 #>  #> --- Transition matrix ---------------------------------- #>   State   ToL1   ToL2   ToL3 #>  FromL1 95.73%  4.27%  0.00% #>  FromL2  2.51% 91.01%  6.48% #>  FromL3  0.00% 11.32% 88.68% #>  #> --- State distribution (observations) ------------------ #>  State Total weight Proportion #>     L1         72.6      35.2% #>     L2         85.2      41.4% #>     L3         48.2      23.4% #>  #> Note: Weights are posterior probabilities. #> ========================================================  # Visualize state information create_hmm_plots(fit)         # Compute QUEST thresholds using the highest state (L3) as the epidemic # state. By default, epidemic_state_indices is the highest state. thresh <- run_threshold_computation(fit)  # Check QUEST threshold information summary(thresh) #>  #> ============================================================== #>         EpiQUEST threshold summary                             #> ============================================================== #>  #> --- Model configuration -------------------------------------- #> Type:                          Continuous (Gaussian)  #> Number of states:              3  #> Seasonal:                      FALSE  #> Number of observations:        206  #> State(s) defined as epidemic:  L3  #>  #> --- Calculated QUEST thresholds ------------------------------ #>      Level Quantile  Value #>        Low       5% 13.881 #>     Medium      70% 19.734 #>       High      90% 21.408 #>  Very high      99% 22.647 #>  #> Note: Thresholds calculated using weighted ECDF #> based on posterior probabilities of epidemic state(s). #> ==============================================================  # Visualize threshold information create_threshold_plots(thresh)
